narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 26, Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st (well below the typical 5th or 6th decile), Year 1 decile of 2nd (also lower than average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your undergraduate GPA, ranked in that order of impact. Your low Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest push toward the failure prediction, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who typically pass. Similarly, your LSAT score of 26—far below the dataset average of about 37—reinforced this risk by indicating preparation challenges common among students who struggle on the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, while below the average of 3.21, worked slightly in your favor, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing compared to even lower GPAs. Overall, these factors made your profile stand out as riskier than typical admitted students, whose stronger later grades and test scores align with bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average levels, strengthening your later undergraduate or graduate coursework to improve decile rankings, and gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Building a clearer path to bar readiness through targeted prep courses could also help in reapplying stronger next cycle. With focused improvements, you can shift the model's view and open new doors.
